draft.introduction: Origen’s Commentary on John (c. 230 CE) is the first systematic commentary on the Fourth Gospel. Book 1 focuses on the Prologue and develops a sophisticated Logos theology, arguing that Christ is the “Image of the Invisible God” through whom all other images derive meaning.

translation.text: The Gospel is the first-fruits of all Scripture, and the first-fruits of the Gospel is the Gospel according to John, whose meaning no one can grasp who has not leaned upon Jesus’s breast and received Mary from Jesus as his own mother. For to become another John, one must become such as John was, designated by Jesus as being Jesus himself.

translator_notes.note: Origen’s hermeneutical principle—that understanding the Gospel of John requires becoming “another John”—is a radical statement about the relationship between spiritual maturity and textual interpretation. Scripture is not a puzzle to be solved by intellect alone but a living word that transforms the reader. This approach profoundly influenced the monastic lectio divina tradition.
